q: A customer-support assistant runs a single chat session for an entire 8-hour shift, accumulating hundreds of turns. As the conversation approaches the model's context window limit, the agent harness performs compaction. Which statement best describes what happens to the session afterward?

options.A: It condenses the older earlier turns into a compact summary and carries that summary plus the most recent context forward, so the agent keeps working without losing the thread.
options.B: The physical context window is temporarily expanded to fit every accumulated turn until the shift finally ends.
options.C: It silently discards the newest messages to free room, retaining only the very oldest turns of the chat.
options.D: The whole session is force-restarted from scratch, dropping all prior knowledge so the user re-explains everything.

explanation: Correct: A — compaction summarizes earlier content and carries a summary plus the remaining recent context forward, so the session keeps going without a restart and the user need not re-explain. B is wrong: the physical token limit is fixed and is not raised or expanded by compaction. C is wrong: compaction keeps recent context and summarizes the old material, rather than dropping the newest messages. D is wrong: compaction exists precisely to avoid a from-scratch restart that would discard prior knowledge.

q: An app sends a 30,000-token product catalog as a stable prefix on every request, followed by a short, ever-changing user question. With prompt caching enabled and requests arriving every 30 seconds, the team sees high cache hit rates and big cost savings. One afternoon traffic drops and 12 minutes pass between two requests. What most likely happens to the second request?

options.A: The prefix's short TTL has lapsed after 12 idle minutes, so the catalog is reprocessed as a miss.
options.B: It is served entirely from the cache, returning the prior response verbatim at zero token cost to the caller.
options.C: The cache is permanent once written, so the catalog prefix is still stored and the request is a guaranteed hit.
options.D: Caching is automatically disabled for that call because the request specified a sampling temperature above zero.

explanation: Correct: A — prompt caching has a short TTL of roughly a few minutes, so a 12-minute gap exceeds it and the prefix expires and must be reprocessed as a cache miss. B is wrong: caching reuses the processed prompt prefix, it does not store and replay final responses verbatim, and the variable question still costs tokens. C is wrong: the cache is not permanent and expires when unused. D is wrong: caching does not require or get disabled by temperature settings.

q: A nightly pipeline must classify 200,000 archived support emails and store the labels in a data warehouse by the next morning; nothing reads the results in real time. The team wants the lowest cost and is fine with multi-hour turnaround. Which approach fits best?

options.A: Submit all 200,000 emails through the asynchronous Message Batches API, which is about 50% cheaper, and correlate each returned label back to its source email using the per-request custom_id field.
options.B: Fire 200,000 synchronous requests in a tight loop to guarantee strict sub-second latency for every single item.
options.C: Concatenate all 200,000 emails into one enormous prompt so the model labels the entire archive in a single call.
options.D: Use the Batches API but poll it every 100 ms, expecting near-instant results so the pipeline still finishes fast.

explanation: Correct: A — the Batches API is about 50% cheaper, allows up to ~24h turnaround, and correlates results by custom_id, ideal for an overnight non-blocking job. B is wrong: synchronous low-latency calls forgo the batch discount and add no value since nothing reads results in real time. C is wrong: 200,000 emails vastly exceed any context window and would destroy attention and accuracy. D is wrong: Batches has no low-latency SLA, so expecting near-instant results misuses it.

q: A legal-research tool has access to a 500-document corpus totaling millions of tokens. For each user query, the team is deciding whether to prepend the entire corpus to every prompt or to retrieve only the most relevant passages. Which design is the sound default and why?

options.A: Retrieve only the relevant chunks per query, cutting cost, respecting limits, and focusing attention.
options.B: Prepend the entire multi-million-token corpus to every prompt so the model can never miss any relevant text.
options.C: Retrieve nothing at query time and rely solely on the model's parametric memory of published legal documents.
options.D: Concatenate the whole corpus once, cache it permanently, and reuse that cache forever at no further token cost.

explanation: Correct: A — retrieval (RAG) of only relevant chunks keeps cost down, stays within context limits, and focuses attention on what matters, the right default for a huge corpus. B is wrong: millions of tokens exceed the window and dilute attention even where they fit, at high cost. C is wrong: relying on parametric memory for a specific proprietary corpus invites hallucination and misses the actual documents. D is wrong: caching is not permanent and has a short TTL, so 'forever at no cost' is false.

q: A team builds a prompt with three parts: a 20,000-token policy manual (identical across all users), a 4,000-token tenant-specific configuration (changes per customer), and a short per-request user message. They want to maximize prompt-cache hit rates across requests from the same tenant. How should they order the content?

options.A: Place the shared 20,000-token policy manual first, then the per-tenant configuration block, then the variable per-request user message last, so the longest stable prefix stays reusable.
options.B: User message first, then the tenant configuration, then the shared policy manual placed at the very end.
options.C: Tenant configuration first, then the per-request user message, then the shared policy manual at the end.
options.D: Interleave the user message between the policy manual and the tenant configuration to keep the blocks balanced.

explanation: Correct: A — caching reuses processed prefixes, so the most stable content (the shared policy manual) goes first, then the moderately stable tenant config, then the variable user message last, maximizing reusable prefix length. B is wrong: putting the variable user message first invalidates the prefix on every request, so nothing downstream can be cached. C is wrong: placing the volatile user message before the large stable manual breaks the cacheable prefix. D is wrong: interleaving variable content between stable blocks fragments the prefix and prevents caching the manual.

q: During a long multi-step agent run, one subtask involves fetching and reasoning over a noisy 15,000-token debug log that is irrelevant to later subtasks. An engineer worries that keeping this log in the shared context for the rest of the run will hurt later steps. What is the best practice?

options.A: Scope the noisy 15,000-token log strictly to the single subtask that needs it, then forward only a brief distilled result to the later steps, keeping the shared context clean.
options.B: Keep the full raw log resident in context for the whole run so every later subtask has maximum information.
options.C: Re-paste the entire raw debug log before each subsequent subtask to guarantee consistency across the steps.
options.D: Raise the model's context window limit for this run so the noisy log never needs to be removed at all.

explanation: Correct: A — avoiding context pollution means confining irrelevant data to the subtask that needs it and forwarding only a concise result, which preserves attention and cost across later steps. B is wrong: carrying a noisy irrelevant log forward pollutes context and wastes tokens. C is wrong: re-pasting it repeatedly multiplies token cost and worsens the pollution. D is wrong: the context window limit is fixed and cannot simply be raised on demand.

q: An agent reads a 25,000-token specification file at the start of a task. Forty turns later it needs a detail from that spec and chooses to re-read the entire file from disk rather than relying on what's already in context. From a token-budget perspective, what is the key consideration?

options.A: The full re-read re-injects all 25,000 tokens, adding cost; reusing context or reading only the needed section is cheaper.
options.B: Re-reading the file from disk is effectively free because file reads never consume any of the context token budget.
options.C: Re-reading is always required here because content already in context is automatically purged after a few turns.
options.D: Re-reading actually reduces total token usage because the new copy replaces the earlier one at no additional cost.

explanation: Correct: A — re-reading the whole file pushes all 25,000 tokens back into context, costing tokens and budget; targeting only the needed section or reusing existing context is cheaper. B is wrong: content read into context counts against the token budget. C is wrong: prior context is not automatically purged every few turns, so a full re-read is not mandatory. D is wrong: re-reading adds tokens rather than replacing the earlier copy for free.

q: A real-time coding assistant must return autocomplete suggestions within a 300 ms budget while the developer types. A teammate proposes routing these suggestions through the Message Batches API to cut inference cost by roughly half. Why is this a poor fit?

options.A: Batches offers no low-latency SLA and can take up to ~24h, unfit for a 300 ms interactive budget.
options.B: The Batches API actually costs more per token than synchronous calls, so there is no cost benefit here to weigh.
options.C: The Batches API cannot correlate results back to individual requests, so the returned suggestions would be misattributed.
options.D: The Batches API only accepts text-classification jobs and is technically unable to generate any code completions.

explanation: Correct: A — Batches has no latency guarantee and may take many hours, so it cannot serve a 300 ms interactive autocomplete need despite the ~50% discount. B is wrong: Batches is about 50% cheaper, not more expensive. C is wrong: Batches correlates results via custom_id, so attribution is not the problem. D is wrong: Batches is not restricted to classification; the disqualifier is latency, not task type.

q: A team enabled prompt caching for a shared 18,000-token system preamble and expected steady savings, but their dashboards show inconsistent, often low cache hit rates. Investigation reveals requests are sporadic — sometimes minutes apart — and that an auto-generated timestamp string is injected at the very top of the preamble on each call. What two factors best explain the poor hit rate?

options.A: The auto-generated leading timestamp changes the very start of the prefix and invalidates it on every call, while the long sporadic idle gaps let the short-TTL cache entries expire before they can be reused.
options.B: Caching only ever takes effect at temperature 0, and these particular requests all ran at a higher temperature.
options.C: The cache stores final responses, so two differing questions can never reuse it regardless of how content is ordered.
options.D: The preamble is far too small to be eligible for caching, and a cache entry never expires once it has been written.

explanation: Correct: A — placing a changing timestamp first breaks the stable prefix so the cache cannot match, and sporadic traffic exceeds the short (~few-minute) TTL so entries expire before reuse. B is wrong: caching does not require temperature 0. C is wrong: caching reuses processed prompt prefixes, not stored final responses, and a stable prefix would help across differing questions. D is wrong: the issue is not size, and the cache does expire via its short TTL rather than persisting forever.
